nodes.name: Russian Literature
nodes.description: Major works of Russian literature and their authors

items.prompt: Who wrote *Crime and Punishment* (1866), following the student Raskolnikov who murders a pawnbroker to test his theory of the 'extraordinary man'?
items.explanation:
Published serially in *Russky Vestnik*, 1866; written under crushing debt, sometimes dictated to a stenographer
Raskolnikov's name derives from the Russian word for 'schism' — his split conscience is the novel's engine
Dostoevsky was sentenced to death by firing squad in 1849 for political activities; reprieved at the last moment and sent to Siberian labor camp
Also wrote *The Brothers Karamazov* (1880), *The Idiot* (1869), and *Notes from Underground* (1864)
His epilepsy and gambling addiction informed much of his fiction's psychological intensity

items.prompt: Who wrote *War and Peace* (1869), the epic novel of five aristocratic families set against Napoleon's 1812 invasion of Russia?
items.explanation:
Published serially 1865–1869; over 580,000 words with 580+ named characters
Tolstoy considered it an 'epic poem,' not a novel; he also wrote *Anna Karenina* (1878) and *The Death of Ivan Ilyich* (1886)
A Russian count who renounced his wealth and title late in life, embracing ascetic Christian anarchism
Influenced Gandhi and Martin Luther King Jr. with his philosophy of nonviolent resistance
Died in 1910 at a remote train station, having fled his estate at age 82 after a domestic crisis

items.prompt: Who wrote *Anna Karenina* (1878), the novel of a Russian aristocrat who abandons her husband for Count Vronsky, opening with 'All happy families are alike'?
items.explanation:
Published serially 1875–1878; Tolstoy considered it his first true novel (unlike *War and Peace*)
Opening line is one of the most quoted in world literature — the 'Anna Karenina principle' used in evolutionary biology
Anna's death under a train is foreshadowed from the novel's opening scene at a railway station
Parallel plot follows Konstantin Levin, widely read as Tolstoy's autobiographical stand-in, seeking spiritual meaning
Tolstoy later renounced his masterpieces, calling fiction a moral waste compared to religious writing

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Brothers Karamazov* (1880), exploring faith, doubt, and parricide through Dmitri, Ivan, and Alyosha Karamazov?
items.explanation:
Published 1880; Dostoevsky's final novel — he died in January 1881, months after completing it
Ivan's 'Grand Inquisitor' chapter is among the most influential philosophical passages in all fiction — a debate about free will, religion, and suffering
Freud wrote 'Dostoevsky and Parricide' (1928) using the novel as a psychoanalytic case study
Addresses theodicy: Ivan challenges God's existence with the suffering of innocent children
Dostoevsky himself was a devout Christian who had faced a mock execution — his faith was hard-won

items.prompt: Who wrote *Dead Souls* (1842), in which the schemer Chichikov travels rural Russia buying 'dead souls' — deceased serfs still on census rolls — as an insurance fraud?
items.explanation:
Published 1842; Gogol originally planned it as a three-part work modeled on Dante's *Divine Comedy* — only Part 1 was completed
Chichikov's scheme exploits the gap between census records (updated every few years) and actual deaths
Satirizes Russian provincial life, bureaucracy, and serfdom with grotesque comedy
Gogol burned the manuscript of Part 2 shortly before his death in 1852, in a religious crisis
Also wrote the short story 'The Overcoat' (1842), whose influence Dostoevsky acknowledged: 'We all came out from under Gogol's overcoat'

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Cherry Orchard* (1904), the play in which an aristocratic family loses their estate — and its beloved cherry orchard — to a self-made merchant?
items.explanation:
Premiered at the Moscow Art Theatre on January 17, 1904 — Chekhov's 44th birthday; he died six months later
Chekhov considered it a comedy; director Stanislavski staged it as a tragedy — a dispute that has continued ever since
The orchard represents the passing of the old Russian aristocratic order, replaced by the newly rich bourgeoisie
Chekhov revolutionized drama: no villain, no hero, no climax — just the texture of lives lived under pressure
Also wrote *The Seagull* (1895), *Uncle Vanya* (1897), and *Three Sisters* (1901)

items.prompt: Who wrote *Notes from Underground* (1864), the novella narrated by a bitter, self-contradicting 'underground man' who rails against rationalism and determinism?
items.explanation:
Published 1864; widely considered the first existentialist novel and a precursor to modern psychology
The Underground Man is the first anti-hero in Russian fiction — petty, contradictory, and searingly self-aware
A direct attack on Nikolai Chernyshevsky's rational utopia *What Is to Be Done?* (1863)
Part I is the philosophical rant; Part II is a humiliating encounter with Liza, a prostitute, that collapses his posturing
Influenced Nietzsche, Sartre, Kafka, and virtually all 20th-century existentialist writers

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Master and Margarita*, the satirical novel in which the Devil visits Soviet Moscow, written in the 1930s but not published until 1966–1967?
items.explanation:
Written 1930s; Bulgakov died in 1940 without seeing it published; his wife preserved the manuscript
Published in censored form in the journal *Moskva* in 1966–1967; full text not published in the USSR until 1973
Woland (the Devil) and his retinue expose Soviet bureaucratic corruption through supernatural mayhem
Parallel plot follows Pontius Pilate and Yeshua Ha-Nozri in Jerusalem — a novel within the novel
The Rolling Stones song 'Sympathy for the Devil' (1968) was inspired by the novel; Jagger read it in 1968

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Idiot* (1869), in which the epileptic, saintly Prince Myshkin returns to Russia and is destroyed by the society he tries to help?
items.explanation:
Published serially 1868–1869; Dostoevsky set out to portray a 'perfectly beautiful man' — and showed how goodness fares in the real world
Prince Myshkin is an epileptic who has lived in a Swiss sanatorium; like Dostoevsky himself, who was epileptic
Nastasya Filipovna — one of Russian literature's most complex female characters — is torn between Myshkin and the passionate Rogozhin
The novel ends in catastrophe: Nastasya is murdered, Myshkin relapses into idiocy, Rogozhin is imprisoned
The title's irony: the 'idiot' is the only moral, honest person in the novel

items.prompt: Who wrote *A Hero of Our Time* (1840), Russia's first major prose novel, following the cynical, self-destructive officer Pechorin through five linked stories?
items.explanation:
Published 1840; Russia's first psychological novel and a foundational work of Russian prose
Pechorin is the archetypal 'superfluous man' — brilliant, bored, destructive, unable to find purpose
The five novellas are told out of chronological order — a deliberate narrative technique to defamiliarize the hero
Lermontov was killed in a duel in 1841 at age 26 — the same fate as his great predecessor Pushkin (1837)
The preface explicitly challenges readers who identify Pechorin as autobiographical: 'the hero of our time is... a portrait composed of all our generation's vices'

items.prompt: Who wrote 'The Overcoat' (1842), the short story about the lowly civil servant Akaky Akakievich, whose new overcoat is stolen and who returns as a ghost?
items.explanation:
Published 1842 in *Arabesques*; one of the most influential short stories in world literature
Akaky Akakievich is a copyist so lowly his colleagues mock him; the overcoat becomes his identity and his ruin
Dostoevsky's famous claim: 'We all came out from under Gogol's overcoat' — acknowledging the story's founding role
Gogol pioneered the 'little man' (malen'ky chelovek) as a subject of serious literary sympathy
Also wrote *Dead Souls* (1842), *The Government Inspector* (1836 play), and 'The Nose' (1836)

items.prompt: Who wrote *First Love* (1860), the novella in which 16-year-old Vladimir falls for the capricious Zinaida, only to discover she is his father's mistress?
items.explanation:
Published 1860; partly autobiographical — Turgenev's own father had a similar affair with a younger woman
The novella is notable for its psychological precision and refusal of melodrama
Turgenev also wrote *Fathers and Sons* (1862), which introduced the word 'nihilist' into Russian literary vocabulary
Lived much of his adult life in Europe, particularly with the singer Pauline Viardot's household
First major Russian author to gain a wide readership in Western Europe; championed by Henry James

items.prompt: Who wrote *Doctor Zhivago*, the novel of a physician-poet caught between love and revolution in early 20th-century Russia, banned in the USSR and published in Italy in 1957?
items.explanation:
Published in Italian by Feltrinelli in 1957 after the Soviet government suppressed it; not published in the USSR until 1988
Pasternak was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1958; Soviet authorities forced him to decline it
Zhivago's poems — Pasternak's own — appear as an appendix and are the novel's emotional core
The 1965 David Lean film with Omar Sharif and Julie Christie became one of the highest-grossing films of all time
Pasternak died in 1960, two years after the Nobel scandal; his funeral drew thousands despite state pressure

items.prompt: Who wrote *We* (1924), the dystopian novel set in a glass-walled future state whose citizens have numbers instead of names — the direct precursor to *1984* and *Brave New World*?
items.explanation:
Written 1920–1921; published first in English (1924) and Czech — never published in the Soviet Union in Zamyatin's lifetime
D-503, a rocket engineer, lives in the glass-walled One State ruled by the Benefactor; all privacy abolished
Orwell read *We* in 1945 and acknowledged its influence on *1984* in an essay
Huxley denied reading *We* before writing *Brave New World*, though similarities are extensive
Zamyatin was allowed to emigrate to Paris in 1931 after Maxim Gorky appealed to Stalin on his behalf; died there in 1937

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Seagull* (1895), the play in which the young writer Treplev loves Nina, who loves the famous author Trigorin, who loves himself — Chekhov's meditation on art, ambition, and unrequited desire?
items.explanation:
Premiered 1895; the initial St. Petersburg production was a catastrophic failure — Chekhov fled the theater
Revived in 1898 by the Moscow Art Theatre under Stanislavski — a triumph that launched both the play and the theatre
The seagull shot by Treplev becomes a symbol of Nina's ruined life — and of Chekhov's technique of symbolic understatement
The Moscow Art Theatre adopted a seagull as its logo in honor of the play's revival
Also wrote *Uncle Vanya* (1898), *Three Sisters* (1901), and *The Cherry Orchard* (1904)
